nuggets.summary: When Dan Lewis quit Amazon to explore a trucking-and-logistics startup idea, he had no industry network, so he manually built one from scratch: he went on LinkedIn, looked up every person he could find who worked in trucking, and cold-messaged them one by one, using each conversation to get introduced to three more people. In parallel he physically showed up at logistics sites - he got buzzed into and then kicked out of a Whole Foods warehouse in Seattle's SoDo neighborhood, and hung around truck stops in Marysville and other locations approaching drivers walking between their trucks and the diner to ask what they liked and disliked about the job. This wasn't polished customer-discovery methodology; it was six weeks of unstructured, in-person grinding that a founder without direct industry access would have had to invent for themselves, and it's the kind of unglamorous, occasionally-getting-kicked-out-of-buildings detail that rarely makes it into startup advice columns.
nuggets.quote: I went on LinkedIn and looked up every single person in the trucking industry and cold in-mailed them. And then they would talk to me and they'd introduce me to three other people. Kept doing that. Got kicked out of the Whole Foods warehouse down in Soto. They just buzzed me in.

nuggets.summary: While simultaneously exploring a second startup idea aimed at college students' mobile shopping habits, Lewis used a specific low-cost field-research setup: he bought a stack of $5 Starbucks gift cards as incentives, printed a paper questionnaire on a clipboard, and deliberately chose a shirt that wouldn't make him look sketchy approaching strangers on campus. He then went to multiple community colleges and interviewed large numbers of students, asking them to physically hand over their phones and walk him through how they shopped app by app. The specific props (gift cards, clipboard, wardrobe choice) reveal the improvised, bootstrapped nature of pre-product customer research that a company deck or case study would never mention.
nuggets.quote: I went and bought a bunch of Starbucks $5 gift cards, got a clipboard, printed, like, a questionnaire, put on a shirt that didn't make me look sketchy. And then went to a bunch of community colleges and just interviewed tons of students

nuggets.summary: The strangers Lewis cold-approached during his six weeks of pre-launch guerrilla research in trucking weren't just data sources - many of them turned into Convoy's actual first customers once the product existed. This let him return to the same people later with a specific, disarming pitch: reminding them they'd once written him off as "the crazy guy" at the truck stop, and now showing them the working product built partly from their own input. This is a non-obvious compounding effect of doing research in person rather than through surveys or secondary data - the research phase itself built the future sales pipeline.
nuggets.quote: Those people I talked to early on became customers later, which is great. Because then you can go back to them and say, hey, man, you're so hopeful to me. You're this crazy guy... Why don't I show you what we actually built?

nuggets.summary: Lewis expected the hardest part of being a first-time CEO to be the sheer number of hours required, since he'd already worked comparably long hours at management consulting firms and big tech companies. What actually turned out to be different - and harder - wasn't time spent but the inability to mentally disengage: as CEO he found he was "never not thinking" about the company, even during time nominally spent with his family. His model shifted from viewing work-life balance as a time-allocation problem to viewing it as an attention-and-mental-processing problem that hours worked don't capture.
nuggets.quote: the mental commitment to it and the position in terms of, especially being, I think, a CEO and a founder is, I'm never not thinking, it's really hard to not think about it, right?... it's not really about time, it's about the attention. How do you not attend to that when you're not at work, right?

nuggets.summary: After becoming a CEO for the first time, Lewis found the experience so different from what he expected that he arrived at a broader belief about human empathy: you cannot actually understand what an experience is like - whether becoming a parent or becoming a CEO - until you've lived it yourself, no matter how much you've observed or been told about it in advance. He explicitly links this to having kids, saying the same realization hit him in both cases. This is a genuine shift in how he thinks about advice-giving and expectation-setting for major life and career transitions, not a generic "it's hard to be a CEO" statement.
nuggets.quote: it actually made me think at one point, I was like, I don't think humans are very good at empathy. I don't think you can actually understand what someone's really going through until you do it yourself in any category.

nuggets.summary: At 22, fresh back from a year living in Chile, Lewis was torn between becoming a Spanish teacher and going into management consulting, with the economy weak after the dot-com bust in 2003. A Yale dean gave him a specific decision rule using the concept of "option value": going into consulting first kept the door open to teaching later, but going into teaching first made a later pivot into consulting unlikely - so when genuinely undecided, choose the path that preserves more future optionality. Lewis says this specific piece of reasoning, not a coin flip or gut feeling, is what actually resolved the decision for him.
nuggets.quote: if you go do management consulting right now, you can have, the odds of you being able to go be a teacher later are really high. If you go do a teaching right now, the odds of being able to go into management consulting later are really low. So, if you're not sure which one you want to do, pick the one that has more option value.

nuggets.summary: In 2015, Lewis and his co-founder built their entire early timeline around a specific fear: widespread speculation in the venture market that funding conditions would tighten dramatically in 2016. Rather than treating fundraising as an open-ended process, they built an explicit work-back plan targeting a credible Series A within eight months of founding the company, treating the funding window itself as a resource with an expiration date. This concrete number - eight months from founding to a fundable Series A story - reflects a real, situation-specific bet on market timing rather than generic "move fast" advice.
nuggets.quote: we had to put in time and energy to get there... we created a work back plan that involved us getting to the point we could go credibly get a series A done in 2015 with basically eight months from finding the company.

nuggets.summary: Before Convoy had a finished product, Lewis set an explicit, numeric milestone rather than a vague mission: get to 100 shipments moved through the fledgling marketplace by a specific date. The number wasn't arbitrary inspiration - it was the threshold he judged necessary to actually understand the business's operational permutations well enough to tell a credible story to Series A investors. Hitting that specific target became the concrete proof point that unlocked the next fundraising round.
nuggets.quote: we're in trucking. We were new. Like, we need a hundred shipments, um, by this date. We didn't have a product. Like, we need to get a hundred shipments at least so we can understand our business enough to go have a really credible story about how to take it to the next level.

nuggets.summary: Many founders write down company values on day one as a founding-document exercise. Lewis asked Jeff Bezos (an Amazon investor in Convoy) directly when and how Amazon created its values, and the answer ran counter to that instinct: Amazon didn't sit down and invent values in the abstract - they waited until the company had grown to roughly 90-100 people, observed what behaviors and practices were actually driving its success, and only then codified those observed patterns into formal values. Convoy deliberately followed the same sequence, trying to write values in week one, concluding "we don't know who we are yet," and shelving the exercise until they had about 30 people and enough shared history to look back on.
nuggets.quote: we didn't just go set the values. We, the company developed and then we saw what was working, right? And then we thought about, okay, what's working, now let's capture those and encapsulate those values into a set of things that we actually live by.

nuggets.summary: A common practice in larger organizations (Lewis names Microsoft explicitly) is to roll individual team goals up into a smaller number of aggregated executive-level goals, so a CEO might track 15-20 high-level goals rather than hundreds of granular ones. Lewis observed that Amazon deliberately does the opposite: goals from small 10-20 person teams roll all the way to the top without being aggregated or summarized, meaning senior leadership is tracking on the order of 500 individual goals directly. He argues this produces real accountability because each team knows a specific person at the top can see their specific number, rather than a "watered-down" version of it - a structural choice he explicitly carried over into how Convoy runs.
nuggets.quote: the goals that a lot of individual teams have, maybe a 10-person team or a 20-person team has, roll all the way up to the top. They don't get aggregated. So it's not like the CEO has 20 goals that he cares about. He's reading like 500 goals and tracking them and measuring against them.

nuggets.summary: Lewis uses an insider account of Amazon's reviews team to illustrate a specific organizational failure mode: the team was originally formed to solve the problem of giving online shoppers confidence to buy without being able to touch or ask about a product, and their solution was customer reviews. But once the team was literally named "Reviews," every subsequent hire and goal became about making reviews go up and to the right - more reviews, more countries, more platforms - and the team stopped revisiting the original problem. It took a deliberate return to the underlying problem (customer confidence) to produce later features like customer Q&A and fit information ("does this run big or small"), which Lewis cites as a concrete case of a solution-named team missing adjacent opportunities.
nuggets.quote: then they called the team reviews. And then everyone they ever hired from there on out was hired to the reviews team. And so every goal they set was how do we make reviews go up and to the right?... if you forget about the problem you're originally solving and you focus on improving the solution, you may actually miss like five other amazing opportunities.

nuggets.summary: Convoy initially assumed truck drivers could be handled like Uber drivers and built its app for Android only, reasoning that most drivers had Android phones and offering to loan an Android device to anyone with an iPhone until an iOS app existed. The hidden flaw was a difference in usage pattern: an Uber driver has their phone in hand constantly because every job is real-time, whereas a trucker is executing today's job while getting notified about tomorrow's - so truckers kept using their own existing phone for calls with dispatchers and wouldn't check Convoy's separate device until the end of the day, since the information wasn't time-sensitive. Recognizing this forced Convoy to build an iOS app quickly to recover, and Lewis frames it as a case where an assumption borrowed from an adjacent, superficially similar business (real-time ride-hailing) didn't transfer to a non-real-time logistics workflow.
nuggets.quote: we assumed we could give drivers phones... we built only for Android initially... an Uber driver is always focused on their phone 100% of the time... Whereas a trucker is doing a job right now, but getting a notification for a job that starts tomorrow from convoy... they're not going to look at the other phone until the end of the day, because it's not real-time.

nuggets.summary: In hindsight, Lewis believes Convoy over-built its initial MVP by fully automating both sides of its two-sided marketplace (an app for truckers/carriers and a web experience for shippers) rather than restricting the initial engineering investment to a single side. His retrospective advice, informed by a machine-learning background, is that founders should pick the one or two highest-priority steps in a workflow to build with real technology up front, and run everything else manually behind the scenes - deliberately using manual, non-scalable processes to validate the scenario before committing engineering and management bandwidth to automating the rest.
nuggets.quote: I may have even, for the MVP, done less. Like, really, like, just picked one thing to automate and to build with technology, and then done that one, maybe just the carrier experience, and then everything else manually... it's completely okay to use manual solutions to a problem up front to make sure you've nailed the scenarios, and to not fragment your management bandwidth and your engineering bandwidth across too many problems at once.

nuggets.summary: When raising Convoy's angel/seed round, Lewis identified team execution risk - whether the founders could actually assemble an engineering team - as one of three things every investor needed to believe, alongside founder commitment and market opportunity. To de-risk this specific concern, he recruited five or six engineers to work on Convoy part-time before the round closed, and put their names, photos, and willingness to serve as references directly into the pitch deck. He states plainly that this specific, verifiable proof of an assembling team - not just narrative or market size - was what made the difference in getting investors to commit.
nuggets.quote: we had five or six people working on this who were engineers and helping us when we wanted to go raise money that was the thing that made all the difference... having the engineering team doing it and committed and like their names on a slide and their pictures and like them willing to be references changed it.

nuggets.summary: Lewis argues that not all venture-backable businesses carry the same kind of risk, and that founders should actively categorize their own business by this distinction when pitching. Some businesses are effectively binary - gaming, messaging, and social networking, in his examples - where nothing short of massive scale and network effects produces any real value, so most attempts simply fail outright (he points to Path as a real example of a social network that got moderately sized and still "didn't really go"). Others, like trucking and logistics, are "linear," meaning effort and diligence translate into proportional, predictable returns rather than requiring a lucky viral spark. He used this specific framing to differentiate Convoy from other pitches in investors' pipelines, arguing it made Convoy look like the comparatively safer bet even in an industry investors didn't understand.
nuggets.quote: there are a lot of businesses that really aren't going to be successful unless they're massive... whereas there are other businesses that are much more linear... trucking for example and logistics I felt like it was less risky because I felt like we could put the energy in and do the homework and really come up with the right approach to it and then work hard to make it happen versus having to kind of get lucky.

nuggets.summary: Right after Convoy closed its Series A, some of its own investors gave Lewis a specific, counter-intuitive piece of advice: write the Series B pitch deck immediately, before there's any real news to put in it, on the logic that if you wait until you feel ready to write it, you may never actually do the next round. Convoy used this to reverse-engineer their post-Series-A roadmap - deciding what story they wanted to be able to tell next and then working backward to the specific proof points (customer category diversity, transaction volume across shipment types) needed to make that future story true.
nuggets.quote: some of the best advice I got even when we were raising our series A as soon as we were done some of our investors when I was talking about it they said write your series B deck right now like if you believe you're going to do a series B.

nuggets.summary: To prepare for the Series A raise, Lewis built a literal two-by-two grid mapping the specific types of expertise Convoy's story would need to be credible - marketplace demand-side experience, marketplace supply-side experience, general marketplace dynamics, and trucking-industry knowledge - and then wrote the name of every person he was recruiting that summer directly onto the grid. This let him track, in real time, whether the people he was courting would actually fill Convoy's specific credibility gaps before the fundraising conversation happened, rather than hiring reactively after the round closed.
nuggets.quote: I actually drew a grid it was like a two by two I was like I need to have people who understand you know demand side of the business in a marketplace, the supply part of business in the marketplace, really understand marketplace dynamics and really understand the trucking industry... I took every person I was speaking with during my recruiting during the summer... and we wrote everyone's name that we were recruiting on that sheet.

nuggets.summary: Lewis says Convoy never seriously considered bootstrapping, and the reasoning was specific to timing rather than generic risk aversion: smartphone penetration among truck drivers, reliable data plans, and accurate GPS coverage in rural areas had only recently become good enough to make an app-based trucking marketplace feasible, meaning the underlying technology enabling the business was itself brand new. Believing this created a limited first-mover window before incumbents or competitors could exploit the same shift, Lewis treated speed as directly convertible into market share ("time equals money") and used venture funding specifically to buy that speed rather than to reduce personal financial risk.
nuggets.quote: truck drivers didn't have smartphones two years ago... because technology allowed that to happen it actually in our business it's going to fundamentally change the way the entire business can be constructed and so we believe that there was a window of opportunity to go after this industry... time equals money to accelerate us getting to market.

nuggets.summary: In explaining Convoy's "know why" value, Lewis makes a specific quantitative argument for why transparency about reasoning matters at scale: he estimates that in a large company like Microsoft, perhaps 10% of employees are the deeply engaged, "diehard" contributors who are two-to-ten times more valuable than disengaged employees who are "just there to get paid," and that moving that ratio from 10% to just 15% would be transformational for the company's overall productivity. This specific hypothetical percentage shift is his justification for building a value around explaining the "why" behind decisions, so employees can exercise independent judgment rather than following instructions blindly.
nuggets.quote: if Microsoft could let's say 10% of their employees are like the super passionate employees let's say they could get that to 15% from 10% that would probably be transformational for their level of productivity as a business.

nuggets.summary: Before ever getting into the field, Lewis forced himself to write out a structured pre-analysis: who all the players in trucking were (drivers, shippers, brokers, transportation companies), what each of them currently pays money for (trucks, software, advice), and then combinations of those into short paragraph-length "user stories" describing hypothetical businesses. Only after narrowing this list through desk research did he go have open-ended field conversations to validate or kill each concept - a process that produced three genuinely distinct possible businesses within trucking alone, from which Convoy's actual model was chosen based on where people said they had unmet needs rather than where Lewis assumed the opportunity was.
nuggets.quote: there were actually three distinct businesses we came up with within trucking and then we picked one of those three and they were very different and that came from talking to a lot of people in the industry and figuring out where people actually needed something versus what we thought they did.
